eli3: The bootstrap (Efron, 1979) estimates the sampling distribution of any statistic by resampling with replacement from the original dataset. Draw B bootstrap samples of size n (each drawn from the original n observations with replacement), compute the statistic on each, and use the distribution of those B values to estimate variance, bias, and confidence intervals — without assuming a parametric model.

A bootstrap sample of size n drawn with replacement from n observations: approximately 63.2% of original observations appear at least once (since P(not selected in one draw) = (1−1/n)ⁿ → 1/e ≈ 0.368 as n→∞), the rest appear 0 times. This means ≈ 36.8% of observations are out-of-bag (OOB) — never selected. OOB observations form a natural validation set, used in random forests.

The bootstrap works because the empirical distribution F̂ₙ (putting mass 1/n on each observation) approximates the unknown true distribution F. Bootstrap resampling from F̂ₙ mimics sampling from F. It fails when F̂ₙ is a poor approximation of F: small samples, heavy tails, extremes (min, max), and non-smooth functionals.

whenWhy.title: Standard errors for complex estimators
whenWhy.body: For statistics without tractable closed-form SE (median, trimmed mean, correlation, Gini coefficient, model predictions), the bootstrap provides a straightforward SE estimate: SE_boot = std({θ̂*_b}_{b=1}^B). No analytical formula needed. B = 200–1000 bootstrap samples is typically sufficient for SE estimation; B = 1000–10,000 for confidence intervals.

whenWhy.title: Confidence intervals without normality
whenWhy.body: Four main bootstrap CI types: (1) Normal: θ̂ ± z_{α/2}·SE_boot (assumes normality); (2) Percentile: [q_{α/2}, q_{1−α/2}] of {θ̂*_b}; (3) Basic (pivot): [2θ̂−q_{1−α/2}, 2θ̂−q_{α/2}] — corrects for bias in the percentile interval; (4) BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated): adjusts for both bias and acceleration (non-constant SE). BCa has the best theoretical properties (second-order accurate) and should be the default.

whenWhy.title: Model selection and cross-validation
whenWhy.body: .632 bootstrap estimator: error estimate = 0.632 · OOB_error + 0.368 · training_error. Better than leave-one-out CV for small samples because OOB sets have different sizes. Used in random forests for honest error estimation without a separate test set. The bootstrap's OOB mechanism is the basis for random forest's built-in validation.

whenWhy.title: Hypothesis testing via bootstrap
whenWhy.body: The permutation test (label shuffling) tests H₀: no difference between groups without parametric assumptions. The bootstrap can also be used for hypothesis testing via the bootstrap p-value: compute the statistic under the null (shift bootstrap distribution to have mean 0), then compute P(|θ̂*| ≥ |θ̂|). This is the two-sample bootstrap test, equivalent to the permutation test for large B.

deepDiveIntro: The nonparametric bootstrap works by treating your observed data as if it were the full population, then simulating the sampling process by drawing samples with replacement. For each bootstrap sample you compute the statistic of interest. The spread of those B estimates approximates the sampling variability you would see if you drew many fresh samples from the real population.

The BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated) confidence interval is the most accurate bootstrap CI method. It corrects for two sources of error: bias (the bootstrap distribution may be systematically shifted from the true distribution) and acceleration (the spread of the bootstrap distribution may change with the parameter value). The corrections are computed from the bootstrap samples plus a set of jackknife (leave-one-out) estimates.

deepDive.heading: Nonparametric Bootstrap: SE and Bias
deepDive.body: The standard error from bootstrapping is simply the standard deviation of the statistic computed across all B samples. The bias is estimated as the average of the bootstrap statistics minus the original estimate. You can use this bias estimate to correct the original estimate by subtracting the measured bias. Around 200 bootstrap samples suffice for a stable standard error, while 1000 or more are needed for reliable confidence interval endpoints. [1]

deepDive.heading: Bootstrap CI Types and Accuracy
deepDive.body: The simplest approach — the percentile interval — just takes the lower and upper quantiles of the bootstrap distribution directly. The basic (pivot) interval flips those quantiles around the original estimate to correct for bias. BCa is the most accurate because it corrects for both bias and for the fact that the bootstrap spread may not be constant — it achieves coverage error that shrinks faster with sample size. For skewed or biased statistics, BCa is substantially more reliable than the simple percentile interval. [1]

deepDive.heading: When Bootstrap Fails
deepDive.body: The bootstrap works by approximating the population with the empirical distribution of your sample. When that approximation breaks down, so does the bootstrap. It fails for the sample maximum (because the extreme value distribution is not well-approximated by resampling), for very small samples (fewer than 10 observations), and for heavy-tailed distributions with infinite variance. It also fails near the unit root boundary in time series, where the special Dickey-Fuller distribution cannot be recovered by standard resampling. [1]

deepDive.heading: Parametric Bootstrap
deepDive.body: Instead of resampling from the raw data, the parametric bootstrap first fits a parametric model and then draws new samples from that fitted model. This can be much more efficient when the model is correct — you are using all the structure of the model to generate realistic samples. The downside is that if the model is wrong, the bootstrap will be biased in a way that is hard to detect. It is commonly used for model checking: compare the observed statistic to its distribution under the fitted model to see whether the model fits. [1]

takeaways: Bootstrap: resample n observations with replacement B times, compute statistic on each. SE_boot = std({θ̂*_b}). [1]
~63.2% of original observations appear in each bootstrap sample; 36.8% are out-of-bag. [1]
BCa CI is second-order accurate — the default for production use. Percentile CI is first-order. [1]
Bootstrap fails for sample extrema, unit roots, very small n, and heavy-tailed distributions (no finite variance). [1]
Parametric bootstrap: resample from fitted model — more efficient if model is correct, biased if misspecified. [1]

edgeCases.q: How many bootstrap samples B are needed?
edgeCases.a: B = 200–500: sufficient for SE estimation (SE of SE ≈ SE/√(2B)). B = 1000–2000: stable percentile CI endpoints. B = 10,000+: stable BCa CI (which is more sensitive to the tails). For hypothesis testing with target power: B should satisfy P(false conclusion) ≤ 0.01, typically requiring B ≥ 999 (so p-values are k/B for k ∈ {0,1,…,B}). Davison & Hinkley (1997) provide detailed guidance. [1]

edgeCases.q: Is the bootstrap valid for dependent data (time series)?
edgeCases.a: Standard iid bootstrap is invalid for time series — resampling breaks dependence structure. Block bootstrap: sample blocks of consecutive observations of length l, then concatenate. Circular block bootstrap (wraps around at the ends) corrects for end-effects. Stationary bootstrap (Politis & Romano, 1994): random block lengths geometrically distributed with mean l. Model-based bootstrap for ARIMA: fit model, resample residuals, reconstruct series. Choice of block length l matters (bias-variance tradeoff in DEFF). [1]

sources.label: Efron & Hastie — Computer Age Statistical Inference, Ch. 10–12
Davison & Hinkley — Bootstrap Methods and Their Application, Ch. 2–5
